1:
"And the Philistines took the ark of God, and brought it from Ebenezer unto Ashdod." The very brief verse notes the Philistines bringing the captured ark to Ashdod. The grammatical voices note the carrying. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "the Philistines took the ark of God, and brought it from Ebenezer unto Ashdod" — the captured ark was carried in triumph to Ashdod; the and the Philistines took the ark of God (the ark taken); the and brought it from Ebenezer unto Ashdod (carried to Ashdod); the figure (the holy ark borne as a trophy into the city of the idol, the seeming triumph of the heathen; the truth that the Philistines brought the ark to Ashdod in triumph, Judges 16:23; the figure of the seeming victory of the foe over the things of God; the carrying of the ark; the ark at Ashdod, vv.4:11). The verse holds together the carrying. The verse displays the apparent triumph of the Philistines, who bore the captured ark into Ashdod, the chief seat of their god Dagon, as the trophy of their victory. The verse displays the truth that God may suffer his cause to seem defeated, only to vindicate his own honour in the house of his enemies.

On "And the Philistines took the ark of God," the witnesses note the capture. Gill notes the Philistines taking the ark (the trophy, Judges 16:23) — the capture (took the ark).

On "and brought it from Ebenezer unto Ashdod," the grammatical-historical Ellicott and grammatical-historical note the carrying: "brought it from Ebenezer unto Ashdod" — the ark carried to the city of Dagon (the seeming triumph); the figure (the seeming victory of the foe over the things of God); the providence (the carrying, Dagon falling before the ark to follow, vv.2-4). The verse displays the ark brought to Ashdod.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the carrying — "the Philistines took the ark of God, and brought it from Ebenezer unto Ashdod," the carrying (Keil, Gill); "And the Philistines took the ark of God," the capture (Judges 16:23, Gill); "and brought it from Ebenezer unto Ashdod," the carrying (Ellicott, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the carrying. The verse marks the Philistines: carrying. The verse displays the ark: a trophy. The verse displays Ashdod: the city of Dagon. The verse displays the triumph: seeming. The carrying displays the seeming victory of the foe over the things of God. The verse displays the foe: exulting. The verse displays the honour of God: at stake. The verse displays the vindication: to come. The ark brought to Ashdod displays the seeming victory of the foe over the things of God: the Philistines took the ark of God, and brought it from Ebenezer unto Ashdod (Judges 16:23) — the captured ark carried in triumph to Ashdod, the seeming victory of the foe over the things of God. The verse displays the ark set by Dagon, and Dagon fallen before it (vv.2-3).
2:
"When the Philistines took the ark of God, they brought it into the house of Dagon, and set it by Dagon." The very brief verse notes the Philistines setting the ark in the house of Dagon. The grammatical voices note the setting. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "the Philistines took the ark of God, they brought it into the house of Dagon, and set it by Dagon" — they placed the ark in their idol's temple as a captive before their god; the when the Philistines took the ark of God, they brought it into the house of Dagon (into the temple); the and set it by Dagon (set by the idol); the figure (the ark set as a captive before the idol, the LORD seemingly subjected to Dagon; the truth that they set the ark before Dagon as in homage, Daniel 1:2; the figure of the heathen ascribing their victory to their idol; the setting of the ark; the ark by Dagon, vv.1). The verse holds together the setting.

On "When the Philistines took the ark of God, they brought it into the house of Dagon," the witnesses note the temple. Gill notes the ark brought into Dagon's house (the homage, Daniel 1:2) — the temple (the house of Dagon).

On "and set it by Dagon," the grammatical-historical Ellicott and grammatical-historical note the placing: "set it by Dagon" — the ark set as a captive before the idol (the seeming homage); the figure (the heathen ascribing their victory to their idol); the providence (the setting, Dagon falling before the ark to follow, vv.3-4). The verse displays the ark set by Dagon.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The verse displays Dagon fallen on his face before the ark (vv.3).
3:
"And when they of Ashdod arose early on the morrow, behold, Dagon was fallen upon his face to the earth before the ark of the LORD. And they took Dagon, and set him in his place again." The very brief verse notes Dagon found fallen before the ark, and set up again. The grammatical voices note the falling. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Dagon was fallen upon his face to the earth before the ark of the LORD. And they took Dagon, and set him in his place again" — the idol was found prostrate before the ark, and the people raised it up; the and when they of Ashdod arose early on the morrow, behold, Dagon was fallen upon his face to the earth before the ark of the LORD (Dagon fallen); the And they took Dagon, and set him in his place again (set up again); the figure (the idol prostrate before the LORD, the false god bowing to the true; the truth that Dagon fell before the ark in homage, Isaiah 46:1; the figure of the idol that must bow before the living God; the falling of Dagon; the idol prostrate, vv.2). The verse holds together the falling.

On "And when they of Ashdod arose early on the morrow, behold, Dagon was fallen upon his face to the earth before the ark of the LORD," the witnesses note the falling. Gill notes Dagon fallen prostrate before the ark (the homage, Isaiah 46:1) — the falling (fallen on his face).

On "And they took Dagon, and set him in his place again," the grammatical-historical Ellicott and grammatical-historical note the raising: "they took Dagon, and set him in his place again" — the people raising the fallen idol (the vain restoring); the figure (the idol that must bow before the living God); the providence (the falling, Dagon broken in pieces to follow, vv.4). The verse displays Dagon fallen before the ark.

The verse displays the falling. The verse marks Dagon: fallen. The verse displays the ark: before which he fell. The verse displays the idol: prostrate. The verse displays the people: raising him. The verse displays Dagon found again fallen and broken, head and hands cut off (vv.4).
4:
"And when they arose early on the morrow morning, behold, Dagon was fallen upon his face to the ground before the ark of the LORD; and the head of Dagon and both the palms of his hands were cut off upon the threshold; only the stump of Dagon was left to him." The witnesses note Dagon found fallen a second time, his head and hands broken off before the ark — the crux of the living God's supremacy over the idols, that the false gods must fall and be broken before him. On "the head of Dagon and both the palms of his hands were cut off upon the threshold; only the stump of Dagon was left," interpreters expound the breaking. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the triumph of the LORD over the idol — "Dagon was fallen upon his face to the ground before the ark of the LORD; and the head of Dagon and both the palms of his hands were cut off... only the stump of Dagon was left": the idol is not merely fallen but mutilated, head and hands broken off, leaving only the trunk; the false god proved powerless in his own house; the LORD's supremacy displayed before the heathen); the lesson — that the LORD will not give his glory to another, and every idol must fall before him, "I am the LORD: that is my name: and my glory will I not give to another, neither my praise to graven images," Isaiah 42:8; the truth that the idols are nothing and shall utterly perish before the living God, "the idols he shall utterly abolish," Isaiah 2:18; the figure — the living God who casts down every false god and exalts himself over all, fulfilled in Christ before whom every knee shall bow and every idol fall, "that at the name of Jesus every knee should bow," Philippians 2:10. The figure of the living God's supremacy over the idols, that the false gods must fall and be broken before him.

On "And when they arose early on the morrow morning, behold, Dagon was fallen upon his face to the ground before the ark of the LORD," the grammatical-historical Keil and witnesses note the second fall. Keil and Ellicott note Dagon fallen again before the ark, the second fall leaving no doubt it was God's hand (the second fall, Isaiah 42:8) — the falling (fallen before the ark).

On "and the head of Dagon and both the palms of his hands were cut off upon the threshold; only the stump of Dagon was left to him," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the mutilation. Calvin and Gill note the idol's head and hands severed, the seat of wisdom and power broken off, only the maimed stump left, the LORD proving Dagon a lifeless block and the false gods unable to stand before him (the idol broken, Isaiah 2:18; Philippians 2:10) — the figure (the living God who casts down every false god); the providence (the breaking, the heavy hand of God on Ashdod to follow, vv.5-6). The verse displays Dagon broken before the ark of the LORD.

The witnesses agree the substance: the idol is not merely fallen but mutilated, head and hands broken off, leaving only the trunk (Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of the LORD's supremacy: the head of Dagon and both the palms of his hands were cut off — the decisive vindication of the LORD's honour, the second fall leaving no doubt, the idol shattered, its head the seat of wisdom and its hands the seat of power severed and cast on the threshold, only the maimed stump remaining, the LORD proving in the idol's own temple that he is the living God and Dagon a lifeless block, the false gods unable to stand but falling and breaking before him, the LORD not giving his glory to another (Isaiah 42:8), the idols utterly abolished before the living God (Isaiah 2:18), every knee bowing at the name of Jesus (Philippians 2:10). The verse displays the supremacy crux. Dagon broken before the ark displays the crux: the head of Dagon and both the palms of his hands were cut off upon the threshold; only the stump of Dagon was left (Isaiah 42:8; Isaiah 2:18; Philippians 2:10) — the living God's supremacy over the idols, that the false gods must fall and be broken before him, their wisdom and power brought to nothing in their own house. The verse displays the LORD's hand heavy on the men of Ashdod (vv.5-6).
5:
"Therefore neither the priests of Dagon, nor any that come into Dagon's house, tread on the threshold of Dagon in Ashdod unto this day." The very brief verse notes the lasting custom of not treading on Dagon's threshold. The grammatical voices note the custom. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "neither the priests of Dagon, nor any that come into Dagon's house, tread on the threshold of Dagon in Ashdod unto this day" — the breaking on the threshold left a superstitious custom; the therefore neither the priests of Dagon, nor any that come into Dagon's house (the priests and worshippers); the tread on the threshold of Dagon in Ashdod unto this day (the threshold avoided); the figure (the lasting mark of the idol's shame, the superstition born of God's stroke; the truth that the breaking on the threshold made a custom unto this day, Zephaniah 1:9; the figure of the memorial of God's judgment on the idol; the custom of the threshold; the idol's shame, vv.4). The verse holds together the custom. The verse displays the abiding memorial of Dagon's fall, the very threshold where his head and hands were broken becoming a thing the Philistines would not tread upon. The verse displays the truth that God's judgments on the idols leave their mark even in the customs of the heathen.

On "Therefore neither the priests of Dagon, nor any that come into Dagon's house," the witnesses note the worshippers. Gill notes the priests and worshippers of Dagon (the custom, Zephaniah 1:9) — the worshippers (priests of Dagon).

On "tread on the threshold of Dagon in Ashdod unto this day," the grammatical-historical Ellicott and grammatical-historical note the threshold: "tread on the threshold of Dagon in Ashdod unto this day" — the threshold avoided in memory of the breaking (the superstition); the figure (the memorial of God's judgment on the idol); the providence (the custom, the heavy hand of God on Ashdod to follow, vv.6). The verse displays the lasting custom of the threshold.

6:
"But the hand of the LORD was heavy upon them of Ashdod, and he destroyed them, and smote them with emerods, even Ashdod and the coasts thereof." The witnesses note the hand of the LORD heavy upon the men of Ashdod, smiting them with tumours — the crux of the heavy hand of God that falls in judgment on those who dishonour him and his holy things. On "the hand of the LORD was heavy upon them of Ashdod... and smote them with emerods," interpreters expound the stroke. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the judgment of God's hand — "but the hand of the LORD was heavy upon them of Ashdod, and he destroyed them, and smote them with emerods, even Ashdod and the coasts thereof": God smote the Philistines with a painful and humiliating plague for their possession of the ark; the witnesses note that God thus made his power known among the heathen, vindicating his honour not only over the false god but upon his people (the heavy hand of God in chastising stroke; the plague that teaches what the idol's fall did not; the LORD vindicating his honour upon the heathen); the lesson — that the hand of God is heavy and dreadful upon those who dishonour him, and none can bear up under his stroke, "it is a fearful thing to fall into the hands of the living God," Hebrews 10:31; the truth that God will be known in his judgments, making his power felt by those who defy him, "I will get me honour upon Pharaoh... and the Egyptians shall know that I am the LORD," Exodus 14:18; the figure — the mighty and judging hand of God that falls upon his enemies, fulfilled in the wrath revealed against all ungodliness, "the wrath of God is revealed from heaven against all ungodliness and unrighteousness of men," Romans 1:18. The figure of the heavy hand of God that falls in judgment on those who dishonour him and his holy things.

On "But the hand of the LORD was heavy upon them of Ashdod, and he destroyed them," the grammatical-historical Keil and witnesses note the heavy hand. Keil and Ellicott note the LORD turning his hand upon the men of Ashdod after casting down their idol, the heavy stroke of judgment (the heavy hand, Hebrews 10:31) — the stroke (the hand of the LORD heavy).

On "and smote them with emerods, even Ashdod and the coasts thereof," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the plague. Calvin and Gill note God smiting them with a sore and shameful disease throughout the region, teaching by suffering what the idol's fall had not, vindicating his honour upon the heathen (the plague of judgment, Exodus 14:18; Romans 1:18) — the figure (the mighty and judging hand of God that falls upon his enemies); the providence (the plague, the Philistines sending the ark away to follow, vv.7-8). The verse displays the LORD's heavy hand on the men of Ashdod.

The witnesses agree the substance: God smote the Philistines with a painful and humiliating plague for their possession of the ark (Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of the heavy hand: the hand of the LORD was heavy upon them of Ashdod — the LORD, having cast down their idol, turning his hand upon the worshippers, smiting Ashdod and all the region with a sore and shameful disease, so that the Philistines learned by suffering what they would not learn by Dagon's fall, that the God of Israel is a living and terrible God who will not be dishonoured, his hand heavy upon those who hold his ark in contempt, God making his power known among the heathen, none able to bear up under his stroke (Hebrews 10:31), God known in his judgments and felt by those who defy him (Exodus 14:18), the wrath revealed against all ungodliness (Romans 1:18). The verse displays the heavy-hand crux. The LORD's heavy hand on the men of Ashdod displays the crux: the hand of the LORD was heavy upon them of Ashdod... and smote them with emerods (Hebrews 10:31; Exodus 14:18; Romans 1:18) — the heavy hand of God that falls in judgment on those who dishonour him and his holy things, teaching by suffering what the idol's fall did not, vindicating his honour upon the heathen. The verse displays the men of Ashdod sending the ark away from them (vv.7-8).
7:

The very brief verse notes the men of Ashdod resolving to send the ark away. The grammatical voices note the resolve. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "the men of Ashdod saw that it was so, they said, The ark of the God of Israel shall not abide with us: for his hand is sore upon us, and upon Dagon our god" — the Ashdodites would not keep the ark, feeling God's hand; the and when the men of Ashdod saw that it was so, they said, The ark of the God of Israel shall not abide with us (not abide with us); the for his hand is sore upon us, and upon Dagon our god (his hand sore); the figure (the heathen owning God's hand yet not turning to him, only seeking to be rid of him; the truth that the Philistines felt God's hand but only sought to remove the ark, Psalm 78:34; the figure of those who feel God's judgment yet seek only to escape it; the resolve of Ashdod; the ark sent away, vv.6). The verse holds together the resolve.

10:
